patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hello?"] Hello. Can you hear me well?
[Clinician asked: "Uh uh yes. I think. It's a bit better. It's a bit, it's a bit, it's not very clear. But  let's continue anyway."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Uh, OK. Let's start again. So how can I help you sir?"] Yes. So, it's been a few days now. I have like a sore, and a red skin. It's kind of, it's really itchy, and it's like super annoying. So I'd like to find something quick to solve it.

[Clinician asked: "OK. No, no problem. I'm happy to help. Um whereabouts in your skin is it affected?"] Uh, mostly like my chest, my, my hands, my arms. Like, like really, it's it's super annoying. Like it's itching a lot, like all the time. And I can't even sleep at night.  I really need something quickly to, to solve it. Because even at work I, I can, when I'm in a meeting and I have to, like uh think about my work, I can't focus, I can't actually focus on my work. It's really annoying because I can't actually think about, uh, what I have to say. I'm always like, uh, disturbed by this disease.

[Clinician asked: "Yeah. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> No no, OK. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> I'm certain  this is obviously affecting you. And uh we'll try our very best to get get this sorted out for you. Um, have you had anything like this before in the past?"] Um so yes. Uh earlier I was like prescribed for my eczema.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] And <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> they gave me like some cream, and something to, uh when I like  shower, when I was in the shower, had to put something. But um

[Clinician asked: "OK. Did it help?"] Um I mean, at that time yes. That's, those symptoms, like these symptoms appear like, when the symptoms appeared again, I tried those and it didn't work. I've tried a few things. Like I bought a, a steroid cream at the pharmacy last night. But it apparently didn't help, because it's still itching a lot today. Uh, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. Do you remember the name of the cream you bought?"] A steroid cream.

[Clinician asked: "OK, steroid, OK.  um  would you say these symptoms are very similar to your eczema symptoms, or different?"] Um it's much more like itchy. And my eczema was more like only in the arm. But now it's  also on the chest. And in the on the, on the hands as well.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Like pretty, yeah when, like I like, for instance hiking, during the weekend. And

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] I am, I can't really do it anymore, because it's like very  like I wanted to do that last weekend. And because super painful, and I I have to take like showers every day to be able to, cannot uh ease this itching part, which is very very annoying.
[Clinician asked: "Yes. No.  Well OK. That's  so you mentioned the itchiness. Um you also mentioned it's been quite sore."] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Um have you, have you noticed any uh bleeding or discharge from your skins?"] . OK. No. Um so my skin is a bit cracked in some parts, like kind of cracked, if you see what I mean. But uh, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. You've not, haven't uh seen any other uh pus or blood, coming out of your skin."] No.

[Clinician asked: "No. And you mentioned it all started three days ago. Are you, four days ago. Are you aware of any triggers, or anything that you may have done, that may have caused your symptoms to start?"] Four days ago.

[Clinician asked: "So for example, have you changed your uh shower gel, any clothing? Have you been around anyone else?"] No I . It's actually like, it's really annoying, because I'm thinking about,  maybe I should change something. Because I I haven't, and I don't understand  I cannot, um afraid of asking people around me because it's cannot you know um, I don't feel really comfortable asking about these questions. It's kind of intimate that. So I <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> your help now.

[Clinician asked: "Mm. Mm. No no, OK. That's OK. Um Fine, so just to resummarize. For the last four days, you've had very itchy skin, very sore skin all over your body, mainly your chest. And did you also mention your arms and legs as well?"] Yeah. Uh, no. Just the chest, hands and like, like inside the elbows. Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK.  anything on your face at all?"] Am I what?
[Clinician asked: "On your face?"] On my face? No.
[Clinician asked: "No, OK. And apart from this, any other symptoms have you noticed? For example, have you noticed any temperature or fevers?"] No.  just very itchy. Nothing else.
[Clinician asked: "Itchy. OK. So your, your bowels are working OK? Your chest is OK? No cough, no breathing difficulties?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "You're passing urine OK."] I I think so.
[Clinician asked: "OK, alright. Um It's always good to know. So, I mean, do you have any ideas as to what could be causing your symptoms?"] Any what?

[Clinician asked: "Any ideas, as to what could be causing your symptoms?"] Um  so I had eczema before. So maybe it's disease, but it seems like more uh, like uh my eczema was only on the arms. So, I'm I'm not sure. I I really have no idea.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. That's OK. So um, normally at this stage I like to examine you. Um To see the rash itself, to see exactly what type of rash it is. But from what you've told me, you mentioned it's, you've got some dry skin and some, uh cracked skin as well. Uh and has been very itchy. And so "] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Uh your your background of eczema. Um So Sorry to say, there's a couple of questions I wanted to ask you, which I completely forgot, my apologies. Um, apart from the eczema, do you have any other medical problems I should be aware of?"] Uh, no. I just had asthma in the past. But not anymore.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Not anymore, OK. And do you take any regular medications at the moment?"] Uh no.
[Clinician asked: "No, OK. Do you have any allergies to anything?"] I  like some , sorry? Yeah?
[Clinician asked: "sorry I said do you have any allergies to anything? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Hello?"] Hello? Yes.
[Clinician asked: "can you hear me? That's alright I think it . Uh do you have any allergies at all?"] Ohh, allergies. Uh, no.

[Clinician asked: "No, OK. Um and tell me about your um uh your situation at home. Who do you live with?"] Uh so, I live with a few flatmates. I'm a, so you know I'm thirty one. Uh I, yeah I'm I have a full time job. And so I'm living with a few people to save money.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And what do you do for work?"] Uh I work uh at a pharmaceutical company. So it's been a, it's been a few years now. Uh it's uh, it's kind of interesting. We're working I'm a, like a kind of project manager. Uh I mean, do you want to, to know more about it?
[Clinician asked: "OK. Uh no no no that's , that's absolutely fine. Um just a couple of questions I want to ask you, which can sometimes affect your symptoms, is, do you smoke at all?"] No.

[Clinician asked: "No. And do you drink much in the way of alcohol?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Uh you know,  once in a while I can, I like hanging out with people once in a while. So yeah I'd say a few, a few beers per week. Something like this. Nothing, nothing crazy. I used to drink a lot more when I was younger. But it's been, it's been a while.

[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. OK I've got to say, at this stage I've got to say  this sound quality is not uh great. But I will continue because we've come this far. Um So um, based on everything you mentioned Sarah ,  I do wonder whether this is a flare-up of your eczema. Um"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "The reason being that you said it was quite itchy. Um it's mainly affecting your chest and your back. Um and uh, having looked at your skin, uh the the the rash does appear quite familiar. Um you mentioned  using steroid creams, yesterday."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "Um from the pharmacy. I wonder whether giving you a stronger prescription of a  of a steroid may be beneficial. Uh, which I'm happy to prescribe to you today. As well as, I'm going to give you something, some emollients, which helps to moisturize the skin."] OK. OK.
[Clinician asked: "Um which  use in the bath and shower. And it's definitely worth um using that for the first seven to ten days."] OK.
[Clinician asked: "Uh before we make the decision on whether it's working or not."] OK.
[Clinician asked: "Um it's also worth um using antihistamines, which you can sometimes buy over counter. Things like Loratadine or Piriton, which can help the itchiness of your skin."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "And uh, um it's also worth keeping a diary of any triggers which are affecting your symptoms, anything you wear or anything you use, in terms of shower gels or soap. Because if that's causing your symptoms, then we need to have a discussion about an eczema CU."] OK. Yes, I have tried antihistamines, uh lately, but it didn't really help.
[Clinician asked: "Ohh, could you say that again, sorry?"] I have tried uh antihistamines, but uh but it didn't really help.

[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. So it's something for you to think about.  you can get different types of antihistamines. I can give you something a little bit stronger today as well. Um, something like Fexofenadine, which I can give to you today. It's definitely worth trying, and it's not going to do you any harm."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um but I  think using the steroids and the emollients, um on a regular basis Uh over the next week to ten days, should hopefully  control your symptoms. But do come back and see me next week, if things don't get better."] That sounds good.
[Clinician asked: "OK? Um do you have any questions for me?"] Uh, no that's it. Thank you very much. Bye. Thank you as well. Bye.
gold_note:
4/7 hx of dry itchy skin, mainly on chest and back. Skin is also sore and cracked. No bleeding or discharge. No spread elsewhere. Not aware of any triggers. Used steroid cream OTC last night with minimal effect. Pt has also tried antihistamines. 
Systemically well otherwise - no fevers, no vomiting, no SOB/cough. No bowel sx. 

PMH: Eczema, Asthma 
DHx: Nil regular
NKDA
SH: lives with flatmates, works as a pharmaceutical manager. Non-smoker, social EtOH. 
ICE: pt thinks might be related to eczema. No concerns

Imp: flare up of eczema

Plan:
1.	Steroid cream - Betnovate BD
2.	Emollients - cetraben - use up to QDS
3.	Trial antihistamines
4.	Keep trigger diary 
5.	Review in 10-14d if symptoms no better, or earlier if any concerns/worsening sx

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
4/7 hx of dry itchy skin
Eczema
Asthma
Steroid cream
Emmolients

naive.response_text:
I understand how frustrating and uncomfortable this must be for you. The constant itching and the way it's affecting your daily life and work must be really challenging. I know how important it is to find some relief quickly.

We discussed a plan to help manage your symptoms. I’ll prescribe a stronger steroid cream to reduce inflammation and help with the itching. Using this along with moisturizers during your baths or showers should help soothe your skin. I also mentioned antihistamines, which can sometimes provide additional relief from the itching. If these don’t work, we can explore other options together.

It’s important to avoid anything that might be triggering your skin, so keeping a diary could help us identify any patterns. Please remember to follow the treatment plan consistently over the next week to ten days. If things don’t improve, we’ll need to revisit your care.

You’re not alone in this, and we’ll work together to get you feeling better. Let me know if you have any questions or concerns in the meantime. My door is always open.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. I can understand how frustrating and distressing this must be, especially since it's affecting your daily life and work. It's clear how much effort you've put into trying different treatments and thinking about possible triggers, and I appreciate your openness in discussing this with me.

Given your history of eczema and the current symptoms—widespread itching, soreness, and dry, cracked skin—it's possible this could be a flare-up of your eczema, perhaps triggered by something in your environment or routine. Alternatively, it might be contact dermatitis, which can occur from exposure to irritants or allergens. To help alleviate your symptoms, I recommend a prescription steroid cream to reduce inflammation and itching. Additionally, using moisturizers during baths and showers can help soothe the skin. I'll also prescribe a stronger antihistamine to manage the itching more effectively.

It would be helpful to keep a diary of your activities, products used, and any potential triggers to better understand what might be causing this outbreak. Please use these treatments consistently over the next week to ten days and monitor any changes. If the symptoms don't improve by then, we should meet again to reassess.

Before you leave, could you summarize the plan in your own words to ensure you understand the steps we're taking together? This will help confirm that we're aligned in our approach. Let's plan to follow up in one week to see how things are progressing. Please don't hesitate to reach out sooner if your condition worsens or if you have any concerns in the meantime.
